title: The Evolution of Treasury Market Liquidity: Evidence from 30 Years of Limit Order Book Data

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York staff report (SR 827) uses order book and transaction data to construct a daily Treasury market liquidity index spanning 30 years (1991-2021), combining bid-ask spreads, quoted depth, and price impact. The index peaked during March 2020 at levels commensurate with the 2007-09 global financial crisis. Key drivers of market liquidity include announcements, implied volatility, and high-frequency trader presence in the market.
key_points: Authors Tobias Adrian, Michael J. Fleming, and Erik Vogt construct a daily liquidity index for U.S. Treasury securities using bid-ask spreads, quoted depth, and price impact.
The sample period covers 30 years (1991-2021) of order book and transactions data.
The index peaked in March 2020 at a level similar to the 2007-09 global financial crisis, while other liquidity proxies showed lower peaks.
Significant drivers of market liquidity include announcements, implied volatility, and high-frequency trader presence.
The index is more sensitive to short-term liquidity drivers compared to existing liquidity proxies in the literature.
Original publication was November 2017; the source was revised in January 2023.
why_it_matters: This research provides a comprehensive empirical framework for measuring Treasury market liquidity across different market regimes. Market participants, policymakers, and risk managers can use this index to assess real-time liquidity conditions and understand historical patterns during stress periods like March 2020.
market_structure_context: The paper documents structural changes in Treasury market liquidity over 30 years, demonstrating how the liquidity index captures short-term market stress that other proxies miss. It identifies high-frequency traders as a significant factor in market liquidity dynamics. The March 2020 liquidity spike comparable to 2007-09 GFC levels illustrates non-linear liquidity deterioration risks in the Treasury market.
